EU Regulation 2023/956 // Definitive Phase Active

CBAM — EU's
Carbon Border
Adjustment Mechanism

From 1 January 2026, every tonne of steel, aluminium, cement, fertiliser, hydrogen or electricity exported to the EU carries a verified carbon price. Protect your market access with precision advisory.

50t

Exemption Limit
Per Importer / Yr
Strategic Context

What is CBAM and
why does it matter now?

The Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM), established by Regulation (EU) 2023/956, places a carbon price on imports of certain carbon-intensive goods into the EU. It ensures imported goods face the same carbon cost as EU-produced goods under the EU Emissions Trading System (ETS).

After a 27-month transitional reporting phase, CBAM entered its definitive phase on 1 January 2026. Your EU importer must now purchase and surrender CBAM certificates equal to the embedded emissions in goods imported from you.

The correct CBAM cost formula per Regulation (EU) 2023/956 is based on a phase-out of ETS free allowances: 2.5% in 2026, rising to 100% by 2034.

€100/tCO₂ Penalty

Non-compliance leads to uncapped penalties. The obligation to surrender certificates remains even after penalty payment.

Certificate Sale: 1 Feb 2027

CBAM certificates for 2026 imports open for sale via the central platform. First surrender deadline: 30 Sept 2027.

ISO 14065 Verification

All data must be verified by an EU-accredited third party. Year 1 requires a mandatory on-site inspection.

Regulatory Scope

Six sectors in scope. Default emission values.

World-average default values from EU Commission JRC (2023), used as reference baseline for imports without verified data.

Iron & Steel
CN Chapter 72 + 73xx
Pig iron (7201) 2.07 tCO₂e/t
Steel semi-finished (7207) 2.21 tCO₂e/t
HRC ≥600mm wide (7208) 2.28 tCO₂e/t
Stainless steel (7219) 4.08 tCO₂e/t
Screws/bolts (7318) 2.21 tCO₂e/t
Aluminium
CN 7601–7616
Unwrought aluminium (7601) 1.49 tCO₂e/t
Bars & rods (7604) 1.49 tCO₂e/t
Wire (7605) 1.49 tCO₂e/t
Plates/sheets (7606) 1.49 tCO₂e/t
Structures (7610) 1.49 tCO₂e/t
Cement
CN 2523 10–90
Kaolinic clays 0.05 tCO₂e/t
Cement clinker 0.83 tCO₂e/t
Portland cement 0.76 tCO₂e/t
Aluminous cement 0.76 tCO₂e/t
Other cements 0.76 tCO₂e/t
Fertilisers
CN 2808, 2814, 3102/05
Nitric acid (2808) 1.33 tCO₂e/t
Ammonia (2814) 1.69 tCO₂e/t
Urea (3102 10) 1.94 tCO₂e/t
NPK fertilisers (3105) 1.25 tCO₂e/t
Hydrogen
CN 2804 10 00
World Avg (SMR) 9.66 tCO₂e/t
Grey (Natural Gas) 10.50 tCO₂e/t
Blue (SMR + CCS) 2.50 tCO₂e/t
Green (Renewable) 0.50 tCO₂e/t
Electricity
CN 2716 00 00
Grid Factor By Country
Default Value Fossil Origin

Exempt from 50t de minimis rule. Values depend on country-specific generation mix.

Official CBAM cost formula — Regulation (EU) 2023/956
CBAM Certificates (tCO₂e) = Volume (t) × Embedded EF (tCO₂e/t) × Phase-in Factor
CBAM Certificate Cost (€) = CBAM Certificates (tCO₂e) × ETS Price (€/tCO₂e)
Embedded EF
Either verified actual installation data or EU-assigned default values (+10% markup in 2026).
Phase-in Factor
2.5% (2026), 5% (2027), 10% (2028), rising to 100% by 2034.
ETS Price
Weekly average ETS auction price from the previous week. ~€70-90/t.
Sources: Regulation (EU) 2023/956 · Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2025/2621
Implementation Roadmap

CBAM Evolutions

Phase 01

2023 – 2025

Transitional Phase

  • • Quarterly reporting on embedded emissions.
  • • No financial obligation (no certificates).
  • • Default values allowed without limit (regulations apply).
Phase 02

2026 – 2027

Definitive Launch

  • • Mandatory verification by EU accredited verifiers.
  • • Phase-in factor starts at 2.5% reduction.
  • • First annual declaration due 31 May 2027.
Phase 03

2028 – 2033

Scope Expansion

  • • Inclusion of indirect emissions for more sectors.
  • • Gradual phase-out of free ETS allowances.
  • • Possible scope expansion to chemicals/polymers.
Phase 04

2034 Onwards

Full Maturity

  • • 100% CBAM liability (No ETS free allowances).
  • • Full alignment with EU carbon leakage goals.
  • • Mature secondary certificate market.
Strategic Readiness

Compliance
Action Plan

1

Analyze Scope

Map all exports to 8-digit CN codes. Identify precursors and complex vs. simple goods categorization under Annex II.

2

Primary Data Check

Implement actual emissions monitoring (direct and indirect). Relying on default values will trigger the "Markup" hazard.

3

Verify Verifier

Secure an EU-accredited independent verifier. ISO 14065 accreditation is mandatory for the definitive phase.

4

Registry Enrollment

Ensure your EU importers are "Authorized CBAM Declarants". Direct registry access for non-EU producers is recommended.

5

Reporting Cycle

Submit verified emissions data annually. Maintain documentation for 7 years to protect against retrospective audits.

6

Strategic Pivot

Evaluate carbon pricing in your production country to unlock Article 9 deductions and reduce certificate obligation.

Cost Escalation Alert

From 2026, free allowances phase out rapidly. By 2034, 100% of emissions impact your balance sheet. Our verified actual data reduces this impact by ~30%–45%.

Year Liability % Est. ETS (€) Certs Req. Est. Cost Visual Path
The Methodology

How CBAM Costs are Defined

1
Embedded Emissions (E)

Direct + Indirect emissions (Specific emissions per tonne of product) based on verified data or EU default values.

2
CBAM Certificates (C)

Required units equal to (Emissions × Volume) adjusted by the EU ETS Phase-out factor of the current year.

3
Certificate Price (P)

Based on the weekly average auction price of EU ETS allowances. Published every Monday.

Cost Formula (Article 21)
Cost = C × P
Liability Equation
where Certificates (C) = (Vol × E) × (1 - FM)

*FM = Free Allocation Multiplier (decreases from 2026 to 2034)

Competitive Advantage

Why verified actual data matters most.

Hot-Rolled Steel 7208

10,000 Metric Tonnes annual volume
Scenario
Definitive Phase 2026
EU Default Value (No Data)
€43,890

Includes mandatory +10% punitive markup for non-reporting.

Verified Actual Data
€28,000

Based on installation-specific primary monitoring data.

Strategic Impact

Eliminating verified data gaps directly reduces your certificate liability by over 1/3 in year one.

€15,890
Annual Savings / 2026

Non-Compliance Risks.

Failing to provide verified data triggers severe financial and legal enforcement under Art. 26/27.

01
€100/tonne Penalty

Applied to every tonne of embedded emissions not covered by certificates.

02
Import Bans

Mandatory customs denial for any goods from non-authorized declarants.

03
No Liability Caps

Unlike the transitional phase, definitive phase penalties scale without limit.

Verify Your Data Today
Our Capacity

SustainCore CBAM Solutions.

Book Assessment →

Frequently Asked Questions

Everything you need to know about the transition to the definitive phase.

Per Regulation (EU) 2023/956: (1) Certificates (tCO₂e) = Volume (t) × EF (tCO₂e/t) × Phase-in Factor. (2) Cost (€) = Certificates × ETS Average Price. The EF is either your verified installation data or the country-specific default values.
Yes. Importers need verified data in the EU Communication Template format covering the full calendar year. Without this, they pay punitive default values. SustainCore manages this entire data delivery chain.
No. Under Art. 9, only an explicit carbon price (ETS or tax) paid in the production country can be deducted. Voluntary offsets do not reduce CBAM certificate exposure.
CBAM costs will scale dramatically. By 2034, 100% of emissions carry the full ETS price (projected ~€130/t). This represents a ~70x cost increase for many exporters over the next 8 years.
The 50-tonne threshold is assessed at the EU importer level across all suppliers and shipments. If the importer exceeds 50t, all imports are in scope. It is not calculated per individual supplier or shipment.
Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2025/2621 uses a capped +1% markup for fertilisers to limit food-price inflation risk, unlike steel and cement where +10% to +30% punitive markups apply.
